title: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR LOW-MOISTURE FOODS CXC 75-2015

sections.section: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR LOW-MOISTURE FOODS CXC 75-2015
sections.summary: Document title: Code of Hygienic Practice for Low‑Moisture Foods (CXC 75‑2015), adopted 2015, revised 2016 and amended 2018 (title/adoption note).

Explains low‑moisture foods (aw ≤0.85) are associated with outbreaks—primarily Salmonella spp. (and Bacillus cereus)—notes pathogens do not multiply but can persist and be heat‑tolerant in low‑aw matrices, and emphasizes controlling Salmonella in the processing environment via GHPs, hygienic equipment design, maintenance, incoming ingredient control and special attention to products exposed after or lacking pathogen reduction steps.

States the Code provides GMPs and GHPs for controlling microbial hazards in all stages of manufacturing low‑moisture foods, with particular emphasis on minimizing Salmonella spp., while also addressing other potential pathogens.

Defines the Code's scope as GMPs/GHPs for manufacturing low‑moisture foods for human consumption, listing covered commodities (e.g. dried fruits/vegetables, cereals, nut butters, dried dairy/soy protein products, confections, snacks, tree nuts, seeds, spices, specialized lipid‑based nutritional products) and noting milled grain products like flour may be included when not subjected to microbial inactivation.

States the Code should be used with the General Principles of Food Hygiene and other applicable commodity codes, provides cross‑references and footnotes on specialized lipid‑based nutritional products, and notes powdered infant formula is excluded from scope because it is addressed by a separate Code.

Refers to definitions in the General Principles and additional defined terms used here: controlled wet cleaning, dry cleaning, harbourage site (locations allowing residue accumulation and microbial survival), low‑moisture foods (aw ≤0.85), and wet cleaning.

Notes raw materials/ingredients for low‑moisture foods vary widely and microbial hazards differ by commodity, so detailed primary production methods are beyond the Code's scope and primary producers should apply practices that promote safe food while referring to the General Principles and other applicable codes.

sections.section: SECTION IV - ESTABLISHMENT: DESIGN AND FACILITIES
sections.summary: Section heading only for 'Establishment: Design and Facilities' without substantive provisions in this excerpt.
Cross‑references the General Principles of Food Hygiene for location requirements; no additional substantive detail provided here.
Refers readers to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for premises and room requirements; no further substantive text in this excerpt.

Specifies hygienic design, zoning and layout for low‑moisture food premises to control pathogen entry and transfer (e.g., separate raw/pre‑processing and post‑processing areas, physically separate areas after a pathogen‑reduction step, and consider transitional zones for products for susceptible consumers), require dust control via barriers or ventilation/airflow, and minimize introduction/use of water by keeping dry‑cleaning areas dry, locating handwashing/footbath stations and water distribution outside high‑hygiene zones, and designing infrastructure to prevent unwanted water entry.

Requires internal structures and fittings to minimize dust accumulation (especially overhead), eliminate cavities that could serve as microbial harbourage, control condensate with measures such as drip pans or ventilation to prevent product contamination, and fit entry/exit doors to high‑hygiene areas tightly with self‑closing devices where necessary.
Non‑substantive cross‑reference: directs the reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene and contains no additional specific requirements.

Sets equipment design and documentation requirements to prevent establishment of pathogens in equipment (design for dry cleaning or controlled wet cleaning with rapid drying, simple construction, accessibility for inspection/cleaning, elimination or sealing of hollow areas, and hygienic design of buttons/switches) and requires written procedures for equipment acceptance and for cleaning, disinfecting and drying before entry into processing areas, with special attention to used equipment.

Mostly a cross‑reference to the General Principles of Food Hygiene, and additionally requires regular inspection of facility integrity (e.g., bird nests, roof leaks) with prompt correction of detected problems to maintain a sound structure.

States that areas requiring stringent hygiene should ideally have no drains; if drains exist they must allow effective drainage and rapid drying, prevent backflow (especially from lower‑hygiene areas), be sealed during dry processing, and ensure rapid drying where water is used.
Requires low‑moisture food handling and manufacturing areas to be designed for dry cleaning to avoid water use, and specifies that non‑fixed equipment should be cleaned outside the high‑hygiene area.

Requires hygienic design and maintenance of exhaust ducts and vents to prevent condensate, dripping and reverse airflow; mandates measures (filters, positive air pressure) to prevent ingress and movement of dust between zones, inspection/maintenance of filters to avoid harbourage, careful siting/filtration of air intakes to avoid contamination sources, and drying/filtration of any air that contacts product.
Section heading only (SECTION V - CONTROL OF OPERATION) with no substantive requirements in this item.

Refers to the General Principles of Food Hygiene and requires zone‑based hygiene controls with more stringent measures where products are exposed after pathogen‑reduction; emphasizes maintaining dry conditions in low‑moisture operations, minimizing water use, and specifies incident response for moisture incursions (remove water, assess need for increased sampling/testing, stop production if safety is compromised, repair the source, clean/disinfect/dry the area and dispose or recondition affected product as appropriate, and take environmental samples to verify sanitation).

sections.section: 5.2 Key aspects of hygiene control systems
sections.summary: Non‑substantive cross‑reference: directs the reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for key aspects of hygiene control systems.

Recommends that low‑moisture foods or raw materials be treated with validated pathogen‑reduction steps (thermal or non‑thermal) with targets and critical limits determined for the specific food, selection of appropriate strains or surrogates for validation/challenge studies where needed, and requires ongoing monitoring and verification of validated processes with corrective actions when deviations occur.

Refers to CXG 21‑1997 and advises that end‑product testing is limited in verifying hygiene controls, recommends implementing an environmental monitoring program to verify sanitation effectiveness, and requires investigation, increased sampling/testing and corrective actions (including validated reprocessing) withhold/release controls before finished product is released if contamination is suspected.

Requires the highest hygienic controls after a pathogen‑reduction step to prevent recontamination and mandates traffic control between hygiene zones (minimize/strictly control movement), personnel hygiene measures before entry, assignment of dedicated personnel and equipment to high‑hygiene areas, compliance of ingredients added without further pathogen reduction with section 5.3, and appropriate airflow from cleaner to less clean areas where applicable.

Refers to the General Principles of Food Hygiene and requires a supplier approval and verification program for sensitive ingredients (those previously positive for pathogens or implicated in outbreaks or used for susceptible consumers), evaluation/audit of supplier food‑safety programs, periodic receipt testing, and on‑site segregation and hygiene/temperature/moisture controls and measures to prevent cross‑contamination prior to bringing sensitive ingredients into high‑hygiene areas.
Non‑substantive cross‑reference: directs the reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene regarding packaging.
Non‑substantive cross‑reference: directs the reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene regarding water.

Requires preventive maintenance on jacketed temperature‑controlled equipment to detect microfractures and mandates use of potable water in jackets so that, in the event of a leak, contaminated water cannot enter product.

Refers to the General Principles of Food Hygiene and requires managers and supervisors to understand the primary pathogen of concern for their low‑moisture product (e.g., Salmonella), the controls to manage it, and procedures to follow when environmental or product sampling results are non‑compliant.
Non‑substantive cross‑reference: directs the reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene regarding documentation and recordkeeping.
Reference only: directs reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for recall procedures; no substantive recall requirements provided in this section.

sections.section: SECTION VI - ESTABLISHMENT: MAINTENANCE AND SANITATION
sections.summary: Section heading only for ‘Establishment: Maintenance and Sanitation’; contains no substantive requirements or guidance in this entry.
Reference only: directs reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for maintenance and cleaning; no additional substantive content in this section.

Addresses maintenance and cleaning in low‑moisture food operations, requiring timely removal of dust and product accumulation (especially for hygroscopic products) and, during construction or maintenance, measures to isolate the area, control or capture dust, control traffic, maintain negative air pressure, intensify cleaning, avoid wet‑cleaning conditions that promote pathogen proliferation, and apply similar controls when dismantling or repositioning equipment.

Lists controls for ‘controlled wet cleaning’: remove residues by dry cleaning first, use only minimal water, collect/contain water, avoid aerosols and high‑pressure hoses, conduct wet cleaning off‑line or in a dedicated room when possible, disinfect afterwards, fully dry all areas/components, verify drying visually and by environmental monitoring, and stop production if necessary until dry.
When wet cleaning is used, minimize and limit water to specific areas, avoid excessive water and high‑pressure hoses, and prevent tracking water into areas intended to remain dry.

Lists controls for ‘controlled wet cleaning’: remove residues by dry cleaning first, use only minimal water, collect/contain water, avoid aerosols and high‑pressure hoses, conduct wet cleaning off‑line or in a dedicated room when possible, disinfect afterwards, fully dry all areas/components, verify drying visually and by environmental monitoring, and stop production if necessary until dry.

Refers to the General Principles and advises using dry‑cleaning methods for persistent cracks or harbourage sites to keep them dry and minimize microbial risks, noting that water entering such sites can enable microbial growth and increase contamination risk.
Reference only: directs reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for pest control systems; no substantive pest control requirements provided here.
Reference only: directs reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for waste management; no substantive waste management requirements provided in this section.

Requires establishments producing products at known risk for pathogens (e.g. nuts, dry protein products) to implement environmental monitoring (swabs, dust, product residue) primarily targeting Salmonella, with Enterobacteriaceae optionally used as a hygiene indicator, and mandates that when detections exceed operator‑established decision criteria the source be investigated and microorganisms eliminated or controlled.
Reference only: heading for personal hygiene directing reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene; no substantive content in this entry.
Reference only: heading for transportation directing reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene; no substantive content in this entry.

sections.section: SECTION IX - PRODUCT INFORMATION AND CONSUMER AWARENESS
sections.summary: Reference only: heading for product information and consumer awareness directing reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene; no substantive content in this entry.
Reference only: directs reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for awareness and responsibilities; no additional substantive requirements in this section.

Training program guidance: employees (including temporary staff/contractors) should be trained on hygienic practices to prevent introduction and spread of pathogens such as Salmonella in low‑moisture food establishments, including adherence to traffic‑pattern controls and avoidance of introducing water.
Reference only: directs reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for instruction and supervision; no substantive instructions provided here.
Reference only: directs reader to the General Principles of Food Hygiene for refresher training; no substantive content in this section.
Annex heading only: ‘ANNEX I’ with no substantive content provided in this entry.

sections.section: EXAMPLES OF MICROBIOLOGICAL CRITERIA FOR LOW-MOISTURE FOODS WHEN DEEMED APPROPRIATE IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE PRINCIPLES AND GUIDELINES FOR THE ESTABLISHMENT AND APPLICATION OF MICROBIOLOGICAL CRITERIA RELATED TO FOODS (CXG 21-1997)
sections.summary: Guidance on microbiological testing for low‑moisture foods: testing, when properly designed and using validated methods, can verify food safety controls and inform specifications (e.g. for Salmonella) taking into account processing, end use and susceptible populations, but testing cannot guarantee safety due to sampling and statistical limitations and organisms’ heterogeneous distribution, so testing should be combined with validated process controls and trend analysis.

sections.section: Example Microbiological Criteria that May be Appropriate for Low-Moisture Foods If Such Criteria are Deemed Necessary g,h
sections.summary: Guidance on when microbiological testing for Salmonella is appropriate for low‑moisture foods, advising consideration of post‑sampling handling/treatment/consumption, use of CXG 21‑1997 to decide necessity, and that sampling plans may be adjusted based on product‑specific data, moving‑window verification, and documented raw material and process controls to minimize testing.

Table of proposed Salmonella sampling plans for low‑moisture foods: for general and highly susceptible populations it specifies n values (5–60) according to expected change in risk, with all rows using c=0, m=0/25 g and a Class Plan of 2.
